discussion_posts.name: Code2G
discussion_posts.content:
I'm confusion about the definition about the boundary, more specifically the left most node. 
It's defined as "The left-most node is defined as a leaf node you could reach when you always firstly travel to the left subtree if exists. If not, travel to the right subtree. Repeat until you reach a leaf node." And I would expect this applies starting from the root. 

In the first example, tree = [1,null,2,3,4], my understanding of the left most node would be 3, and the left boundary is 1->2->3. 

But seems the definition of 'left most' does not apply to the root node?

discussion_posts.name: ekenk
discussion_posts.content:
# Boundary Definition:

The definition of boundary is not very well defined. Here is a better definition of it (or at least what got me through the problem):

## Left Boundary:
* All nodes in the boundary are non-leaf nodes
* Given that it is not a leaf node, it must have 1 or 2 children nodes
   * If the node has a left child, that left child would be in the left boundary UNLESS it is a leaf node
      * Given that the node has a left child, no right child is in the left boundary
   * If the node does NOT have a left child but does have a right child, that right child would be in the left boundary UNLESS it is a leaf node

## Right Boundary:
* All nodes in the boundary are non-leaf nodes
* Given that it is not a leaf node, it must have 1 or 2 children nodes
   * If the node has a right child, that right child would be in the right boundary UNLESS it is a leaf node
      * Given that the node has a right child, no left child is in the right boundary
   * If the node does NOT have a right child but does have a left child, that left child would be in the right boundary UNLESS it is a leaf node

Hope this helps!

discussion_posts.name: infinitezest
discussion_posts.content:
> The root node's left child is in the left boundary.

> The leftmost leaf is not in the left boundary.

These rules contradict each other if the root's left child is the leftmost leaf. The first rule says it **is** in the left boundary, the last one says it **isn't**. If the rules are contradictory, you need to specify the order of precedence, which currently the problem doesn't as of 2024-05-07.

I understood the problem based only on the examples.

discussion_posts.name: Icarus2000
discussion_posts.content: The definitions for left boundary and right boundary are ill defined since they are applicable only to the root node. A good definition would have worked for any sub-tree of the input. For example an easy test for weather a node belonged to the left boundary or right boundary would simply have been to see if it was the left node or the right node.
